revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
'''Gakkōrei''' (学校令) = A series of five (or four) [[Imperial ordinances]] (standalone edicts) promulgated in [[Japan]] between [[March 2]] and [[April 10]], [[:en:1886|1886]] (Meiji 19), regulating the classifications of primary, secondary, and higher educational institutions. These include the '''[[Teikoku Daigakurei]]''', '''[[Shihan Gakkōrei]]''', '''[[Shōgakkōrei]]''', and '''[[Chūgakkōrei]]'''. = A collective term for standalone edicts regulating different types of schools, promulgated from 1886 (Meiji 19) to the enactment of the [[School Education Act]] in [[1947]] (Shōwa 22), as discussed in this article.
----
'''Gakkōrei''' (学校令) refers to a series of [[Imperial ordinances]] established in [[Japan]] from the [[Meiji period]] onward, replacing the [[Kyōikurei]] and remaining in effect until the enactment of the [[School Education Act]] following [[World War II]]. These ordinances collectively regulated the classifications of schools within the "[[old education system of Japan|old system]]" and the framework of public education. They are sometimes referred to as "'''Shogakkōrei''' (諸学校令)" to emphasize their collective nature.
== Overview == The initial "Gakkōrei" refers to five [[Imperial ordinances]] promulgated between March 2 and April 10, [[:en:1886|1886]]: the [[Teikoku Daigakurei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 3, March 2, Meiji 19), the [[Shihan Gakkōrei]] (Ordinance No. 13, April 10, Meiji 19), the [[Shōgakkōrei]] (Ordinance No. 14, April 10, Meiji 19), the [[Chūgakkōrei]] (Ordinance No. 15, April 10, Meiji 19), and the [[Shogakkō Tsūsoku]] (General School Ordinance, Ordinance No. 16, April 10, Meiji 19). These constitute the "'''narrow definition of Gakkōrei'''" and replaced the [[Kyōikurei=Third Kyōikurei|Third Kyōikurei]].
In its "'''broader definition'''," Gakkōrei refers to the collective term for standalone ordinances that regulated specific school types promulgated sporadically until the [[School Education Act]] of 1947. Unlike the [[Gakusei]] and [[Kyōikurei]] (single comprehensive edicts), the Gakkōrei was not a single legal document but a collective designation for multiple ordinances. To emphasize this distinction, it is sometimes referred to as "Shogakkōrei."
== Development == === Transition from Gakusei and Kyōikurei === Initially, public education in [[modern Japan]] was governed by the [[Gakusei]], established in [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 5), and later replaced by the [[Kyōikurei]] in [[:en:1879|1879]]. These edicts comprehensively regulated various types of schools. However, the rapid expansion of the educational system in the early [[Meiji period]] necessitated significant reforms in the [[:en:1880s|1880s]]. The Kyōikurei underwent complete revisions twice, in [[:en:1880|1880]] and [[:en:1885|1885]], before being replaced by the Gakkōrei series in [[:en:1886|1886]].
=== Mori Arinori and the Creation of Gakkōrei === [[File:Arinori Mori 2.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Mori Arinori, the first [[Minister of Education]], who played a key role in establishing the Gakkōrei.]] [[Mori Arinori]], Japan's first [[Minister of Education]], initiated the creation of Gakkōrei during his tenure. Mori believed that education reform should prioritize national stability and align with state objectives. Emphasizing teacher training, he advocated for the establishment of separate ordinances ("individual regulations") to avoid frequent large-scale revisions.
Consequently, between March and April of [[:en:1886|1886]], five standalone edicts were promulgated: the [[Teikoku Daigakurei]], [[Shihan Gakkōrei]], [[Shōgakkōrei]], [[Chūgakkōrei]], and [[Shogakkō Tsūsoku]]. These ordinances collectively replaced the [[Kyōikurei]] and established the foundation of Japan's [[public education system]] under state control. Mori planned to enact additional ordinances, but his assassination in [[:en:1889|1889]] left his vision incomplete.
=== Subsequent Revisions and Additions === The initial ordinances underwent revisions to adapt to Japan's changing educational needs. These revisions clarified the roles of local governments, redefined primary and secondary education, and expanded higher education. Notable reforms included the [[High School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1894|1894]], the [[Private School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1899|1899]], and the [[Specialized School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1903|1903]]. By the [[Taishō period]], the educational system had become highly structured, culminating in the [[University Ordinance]] of [[:en:1918|1918]], which allowed the establishment of non-imperial [[universities]].
=== Wartime Education and Postwar Reforms === During the [[Shōwa period]], Japan's education system was heavily influenced by wartime demands. The [[Youth School Ordinance]] of [[1935]] and the [[Secondary School Ordinance]] of [[1943]] reflected efforts to mobilize the population. Following Japan's defeat in [[World War II]], the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]] mandated educational reforms, leading to the enactment of the [[School Education Act]] in [[1947]], which replaced the old system with a unified modern structure.
== Related Ordinances (Narrow Definition) == === Imperial University Ordinance === {{main|Teikoku Daigakurei}} Established in [[:en:1886|1886]], the [[Teikoku Daigakurei]] defined the structure of [[Imperial Universities]]. Subsequent revisions led to its renaming as the [[National Comprehensive University Ordinance]] in [[1947]].
=== Teacher Training Ordinance === {{main|Shihan Gakkōrei|Shihan Kyōikurei}} The [[Shihan Gakkōrei]] regulated [[normal schools]] and was later replaced by the [[Shihan Kyōikurei]] in [[:en:1897|1897]] to enhance teacher training.
=== Primary School Ordinance === {{main|Shōgakkōrei|Kokumin Gakkōrei}} The [[Shōgakkōrei]] established the structure of [[primary education]], later replaced by the [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]] in [[1941]].
=== Middle School Ordinance === {{main|Chūgakkōrei|Chūtō Gakkōrei}} The [[Chūgakkōrei]] defined [[middle schools]] and evolved into the [[Chūtō Gakkōrei]] in [[1943]].
=== General School Ordinance === {{main|Shogakkō Tsūsoku}} The [[Shogakkō Tsūsoku]] regulated school facilities, including libraries and teacher qualifications. It was repealed in [[:en:1900|1900]].
== Broader Scope of Gakkōrei == === Specialized School Ordinance (1903) {{main|Specialized School Ordinance}} === University Ordinance (1918) {{main|University Ordinance}}
== References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group=Notes}}
=== Citations === {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AD%A6%E6%A0%A1%E4%BB%A4-464258 Gakkōrei - Kotobank] * [https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317609.htm "Mori Arinori and the Gakkōrei" - Ministry of Education] * [https://www.archives.go.jp/ayumi/kobetsu/m19_1886_01.html "Public Documents on the Gakkōrei" - Japan National Archives]

These revisions clarified the roles of local governments, redefined primary and secondary education, and expanded higher education. Notable reforms included the [[High School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1894|1894]], the [[Private School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1899|1899]], and the [[Specialized School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1903|1903]]. By the [[Taishō period]], the educational system had become highly structured, culminating in the [[University Ordinance]] of [[:en:1918|1918]], which allowed the establishment of non-imperial [[universities]].
=== Wartime Education and Postwar Reforms === During the [[Shōwa period]], Japan's education system was heavily influenced by wartime demands. The [[Youth School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1935|1935]] and the [[Secondary School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1943|1943]] reflected efforts to mobilize the population. Following Japan's defeat in [[World War II]], the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]] mandated educational reforms, leading to the enactment of the [[School Education Act]] in [[1947]], which replaced the old system with a unified modern structure.
== Related Ordinances (Narrow Definition) == === Imperial University Ordinance === {{main|Teikoku Daigakurei}} Established in [[:en:1886|1886]], the [[Teikoku Daigakurei]] defined the structure of [[Imperial Universities]]. Subsequent revisions led to its renaming as the [[National Comprehensive University Ordinance]] in [[1947]].
=== Teacher Training Ordinance === {{main|Shihan Gakkōrei|Shihan Kyōikurei}} The [[Shihan Gakkōrei]] regulated [[normal schools]] and was later replaced by the [[Shihan Kyōikurei]] in [[:en:1897|1897]] to enhance teacher training.
=== Primary School Ordinance === {{main|Shōgakkōrei|Kokumin Gakkōrei}} The [[Shōgakkōrei]] established the structure of [[primary education]], later replaced by the [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]] in [[:en:1941|1941]].
=== Middle School Ordinance === {{main|Chūgakkōrei|Chūtō Gakkōrei}} The [[Chūgakkōrei]] defined [[middle schools]] and evolved into the [[Chūtō Gakkōrei]] in [[:en:1943|1943]].
=== General School Ordinance === {{main|Shogakkō Tsūsoku}} The [[Shogakkō Tsūsoku]] regulated school facilities, including libraries and teacher qualifications. It was repealed in [[:en:1900|1900]].
== Broader Scope of Gakkōrei == === Specialized School Ordinance (1903) {{main|Specialized School Ordinance}} === University Ordinance (1918) {{main|University Ordinance}}

revision.text:
'''Gakkōrei''' (学校令) = A series of five (or four) [[Imperial ordinances]] (standalone edicts) promulgated in [[Japan]] between [[March 2]] and [[April 10]], [[:en:1886|1886]] (Meiji 19), regulating the classifications of primary, secondary, and higher educational institutions. These include the '''[[Teikoku Daigakurei]]''', '''[[Shihan Gakkōrei]]''', '''[[Shōgakkōrei]]''', and '''[[Chūgakkōrei]]'''. = A collective term for standalone edicts regulating different types of schools, promulgated from 1886 (Meiji 19) to the enactment of the [[School Education Act]] in [[:en:1947|1947]] (Shōwa 22), as discussed in this article.
----
'''Gakkōrei''' (学校令) refers to a series of [[Imperial ordinances]] established in [[Japan]] from the [[Meiji period]] onward, replacing the [[Kyōikurei]] and remaining in effect until the enactment of the [[School Education Act]] following [[World War II]]. These ordinances collectively regulated the classifications of schools within the "[[old education system of Japan|old system]]" and the framework of public education. They are sometimes referred to as "'''Shogakkōrei''' (諸学校令)" to emphasize their collective nature.
== Overview == The initial "Gakkōrei" refers to five [[Imperial ordinances]] promulgated between March 2 and April 10, [[:en:1886|1886]]: the [[Teikoku Daigakurei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 3, March 2, Meiji 19), the [[Shihan Gakkōrei]] (Ordinance No. 13, April 10, Meiji 19), the [[Shōgakkōrei]] (Ordinance No. 14, April 10, Meiji 19), the [[Chūgakkōrei]] (Ordinance No. 15, April 10, Meiji 19), and the [[Shogakkō Tsūsoku]] (General School Ordinance, Ordinance No. 16, April 10, Meiji 19). These constitute the "'''narrow definition of Gakkōrei'''" and replaced the [[Kyōikurei=Third Kyōikurei|Third Kyōikurei]].
In its "'''broader definition'''," Gakkōrei refers to the collective term for standalone ordinances that regulated specific school types promulgated sporadically until the [[School Education Act]] of 1947. Unlike the [[Gakusei]] and [[Kyōikurei]] (single comprehensive edicts), the Gakkōrei was not a single legal document but a collective designation for multiple ordinances. To emphasize this distinction, it is sometimes referred to as "Shogakkōrei."
== Development == === Transition from Gakusei and Kyōikurei === Initially, public education in [[modern Japan]] was governed by the [[Gakusei]], established in [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 5), and later replaced by the [[Kyōikurei]] in [[:en:1879|1879]]. These edicts comprehensively regulated various types of schools. However, the rapid expansion of the educational system in the early [[Meiji period]] necessitated significant reforms in the [[:en:1880s|1880s]]. The Kyōikurei underwent complete revisions twice, in [[:en:1880|1880]] and [[:en:1885|1885]], before being replaced by the Gakkōrei series in [[:en:1886|1886]].
=== Mori Arinori and the Creation of Gakkōrei === [[File:Arinori Mori 2.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Mori Arinori, the first [[Minister of Education]], who played a key role in establishing the Gakkōrei.]] [[Mori Arinori]], Japan's first [[Minister of Education]], initiated the creation of Gakkōrei during his tenure. Mori believed that education reform should prioritize national stability and align with state objectives. Emphasizing teacher training, he advocated for the establishment of separate ordinances ("individual regulations") to avoid frequent large-scale revisions.
Consequently, between March and April of [[:en:1886|1886]], five standalone edicts were promulgated: the [[Teikoku Daigakurei]], [[Shihan Gakkōrei]], [[Shōgakkōrei]], [[Chūgakkōrei]], and [[Shogakkō Tsūsoku]]. These ordinances collectively replaced the [[Kyōikurei]] and established the foundation of Japan's [[public education system]] under state control. Mori planned to enact additional ordinances, but his assassination in [[:en:1889|1889]] left his vision incomplete.
=== Subsequent Revisions and Additions === The initial ordinances underwent revisions to adapt to Japan's changing educational needs. These revisions clarified the roles of local governments, redefined primary and secondary education, and expanded higher education. Notable reforms included the [[High School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1894|1894]], the [[Private School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1899|1899]], and the [[Specialized School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1903|1903]]. By the [[Taishō period]], the educational system had become highly structured, culminating in the [[University Ordinance]] of [[:en:1918|1918]], which allowed the establishment of non-imperial [[universities]].
=== Wartime Education and Postwar Reforms === During the [[Shōwa period]], Japan's education system was heavily influenced by wartime demands. The [[Youth School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1935|1935]] and the [[Secondary School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1943|1943]] reflected efforts to mobilize the population. Following Japan's defeat in [[World War II]], the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]] mandated educational reforms, leading to the enactment of the [[School Education Act]] in [[:en:1947|1947]], which replaced the old system with a unified modern structure.
== Related Ordinances (Narrow Definition) == === Imperial University Ordinance === {{main|Teikoku Daigakurei}} Established in [[:en:1886|1886]], the [[Teikoku Daigakurei]] defined the structure of [[Imperial Universities]]. Subsequent revisions led to its renaming as the [[National Comprehensive University Ordinance]] in [[:en:1947|1947]].
=== Teacher Training Ordinance === {{main|Shihan Gakkōrei|Shihan Kyōikurei}} The [[Shihan Gakkōrei]] regulated [[normal schools]] and was later replaced by the [[Shihan Kyōikurei]] in [[:en:1897|1897]] to enhance teacher training.
=== Primary School Ordinance === {{main|Shōgakkōrei|Kokumin Gakkōrei}} The [[Shōgakkōrei]] established the structure of [[primary education]], later replaced by the [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]] in [[:en:1941|1941]].
=== Middle School Ordinance === {{main|Chūgakkōrei|Chūtō Gakkōrei}} The [[Chūgakkōrei]] defined [[middle schools]] and evolved into the [[Chūtō Gakkōrei]] in [[:en:1943|1943]].
=== General School Ordinance === {{main|Shogakkō Tsūsoku}} The [[Shogakkō Tsūsoku]] regulated school facilities, including libraries and teacher qualifications. It was repealed in [[:en:1900|1900]].
== Broader Scope of Gakkōrei == === Specialized School Ordinance (1903) {{main|Specialized School Ordinance}} === University Ordinance (1918) {{main|University Ordinance}}

== Overview == The initial "Gakkōrei" refers to five [[Imperial ordinances]] promulgated between March 2 and April 10, [[:en:1886|1886]]: the [[Teikoku Daigakurei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 3, March 2, Meiji 19), the [[Shihan Gakkōrei]] (Ordinance No. 13, April 10, Meiji 19), the [[Shōgakkōrei]] (Ordinance No. 14, April 10, Meiji 19), the [[Chūgakkōrei]] (Ordinance No. 15, April 10, Meiji 19), and the [[Shogakkō Tsūsoku]] (General School Ordinance, Ordinance No. 16, April 10, Meiji 19). These constitute the "'''narrow definition of Gakkōrei'''" and replaced the [[Kyōikurei=Third Kyōikurei|Third Kyōikurei]].
In its "'''broader definition'''," Gakkōrei refers to the collective term for standalone ordinances that regulated specific school types promulgated sporadically until the [[School Education Act]] of 1947. Unlike the [[Gakusei]] and [[Kyōikurei]] (single comprehensive edicts), the Gakkōrei was not a single legal document but a collective designation for multiple ordinances. To emphasize this distinction, it is sometimes referred to as "Shogakkōrei."
== Development == === Transition from Gakusei and Kyōikurei === Initially, public education in [[modern Japan]] was governed by the [[Gakusei]], established in [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 5), and later replaced by the [[Kyōikurei]] in [[:en:1879|1879]]. These edicts comprehensively regulated various types of schools. However, the rapid expansion of the educational system in the early [[Meiji period]] necessitated significant reforms in the [[:en:1880s|1880s]]. The Kyōikurei underwent complete revisions twice, in [[:en:1880|1880]] and [[:en:1885|1885]], before being replaced by the Gakkōrei series in [[:en:1886|1886]].
=== Mori Arinori and the Creation of Gakkōrei === [[File:Arinori Mori 2.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Mori Arinori, the first [[Minister of Education]], who played a key role in establishing the Gakkōrei.]] [[Mori Arinori]], Japan's first [[Minister of Education]], initiated the creation of Gakkōrei during his tenure. Mori believed that education reform should prioritize national stability and align with state objectives. Emphasizing teacher training, he advocated for the establishment of separate ordinances ("individual regulations") to avoid frequent large-scale revisions.
Consequently, between March and April of [[:en:1886|1886]], five standalone edicts were promulgated: the [[Teikoku Daigakurei]], [[Shihan Gakkōrei]], [[Shōgakkōrei]], [[Chūgakkōrei]], and [[Shogakkō Tsūsoku]]. These ordinances collectively replaced the [[Kyōikurei]] and established the foundation of Japan's [[public education system]] under state control. Mori planned to enact additional ordinances, but his assassination in [[:en:1889|1889]] left his vision incomplete.
=== Subsequent Revisions and Additions === The initial ordinances underwent revisions to adapt to Japan's changing educational needs. These revisions clarified the roles of local governments, redefined primary and secondary education, and expanded higher education. Notable reforms included the [[High School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1894|1894]], the [[Private School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1899|1899]], and the [[Specialized School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1903|1903]]. By the [[Taishō period]], the educational system had become highly structured, culminating in the [[University Ordinance]] of [[:en:1918|1918]], which allowed the establishment of non-imperial [[universities]].
=== Wartime Education and Postwar Reforms === During the [[Shōwa period]], Japan's education system was heavily influenced by wartime demands. The [[Youth School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1935|1935]] and the [[Secondary School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1943|1943]] reflected efforts to mobilize the population. Following Japan's defeat in [[World War II]], the [[:en:Allied Occupation of Japan|Allied Occupation of Japan]] mandated educational reforms, leading to the enactment of the [[School Education Act]] in [[:en:1947|1947]], which replaced the old system with a unified modern structure.
== Related Ordinances (Narrow Definition) == === Imperial University Ordinance === {{main|Teikoku Daigakurei}} Established in [[:en:1886|1886]], the [[Teikoku Daigakurei]] defined the structure of [[Imperial Universities]]. Subsequent revisions led to its renaming as the [[National Comprehensive University Ordinance]] in [[:en:1947|1947]].
=== Teacher Training Ordinance === {{main|Shihan Gakkōrei|Shihan Kyōikurei}} The [[Shihan Gakkōrei]] regulated [[normal schools]] and was later replaced by the [[Shihan Kyōikurei]] in [[:en:1897|1897]] to enhance teacher training.
=== Primary School Ordinance === {{main|Shōgakkōrei|Kokumin Gakkōrei}} The [[Shōgakkōrei]] established the structure of [[primary education]], later replaced by the [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]] in [[:en:1941|1941]].
=== Middle School Ordinance === {{main|Chūgakkōrei|Chūtō Gakkōrei}} The [[Chūgakkōrei]] defined [[middle schools]] and evolved into the [[Chūtō Gakkōrei]] in [[:en:1943|1943]].
=== General School Ordinance === {{main|Shogakkō Tsūsoku}} The [[Shogakkō Tsūsoku]] regulated school facilities, including libraries and teacher qualifications. It was repealed in [[:en:1900|1900]].
== Broader Scope of Gakkōrei == === Specialized School Ordinance (1903) {{main|Specialized School Ordinance}} === University Ordinance (1918) {{main|University Ordinance}}

These include the '''[[Teikoku Daigakurei]]''', '''[[Shihan Gakkōrei]]''', '''[[Shōgakkōrei]]''', and '''[[Chūgakkōrei]]'''. = A collective term for standalone edicts regulating different types of schools, promulgated from 1886 (Meiji 19) to the enactment of the [[School Education Act]] in [[:en:1947|1947]] (Shōwa 22), as discussed in this article.
----

They are sometimes referred to as "'''Shogakkōrei''' (諸学校令)" to emphasize their collective nature.

The Kyōikurei underwent complete revisions twice, in [[:en:1880|1880]] and [[:en:1885|1885]], before being replaced by the Gakkōrei series in [[:en:1886|1886]].
=== Mori Arinori and the Creation of Gakkōrei === [[File:Arinori Mori 2.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Mori Arinori, the first [[Minister of Education]], who played a key role in establishing the Gakkōrei.]] [[Mori Arinori]], Japan's first [[Minister of Education]], initiated the creation of Gakkōrei during his tenure. Emphasizing teacher training, he advocated for the establishment of separate ordinances ("individual regulations") to avoid frequent large-scale revisions.

Notable reforms included the [[High School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1894|1894]], the [[Private School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1899|1899]], and the [[Specialized School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1903|1903]]. By the [[Taishō period]], the educational system had become highly structured, culminating in the [[University Ordinance]] of [[:en:1918|1918]], which allowed the establishment of non-imperial [[universities]].

The [[Youth School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1935|1935]] and the [[Secondary School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1943|1943]] reflected efforts to mobilize the population. Following Japan's defeat in [[World War II]], the [[:en:Allied Occupation of Japan|Allied Occupation of Japan]] mandated educational reforms, leading to the enactment of the [[School Education Act]] in [[:en:1947|1947]], which replaced the old system with a unified modern structure.

=== Middle School Ordinance === {{main|Chūgakkōrei|Chūtō Gakkōrei}} The [[Chūgakkōrei]] defined [[middle schools]] and evolved into the [[Chūtō Gakkōrei]] in [[:en:1943|1943]].
=== General School Ordinance === {{main|Shogakkō Tsūsoku}} The [[Shogakkō Tsūsoku]] regulated school facilities, including libraries and teacher qualifications. It was repealed in [[:en:1900|1900]].
== Broader Scope of Gakkōrei == === Specialized School Ordinance (1903) {{main|Specialized School Ordinance}} === University Ordinance (1918) {{main|University Ordinance}}

revision.comment: Merge [[Category:19世紀日本の教育]] into [[Category:19th Century Education in Japan]] via bot
revision.text:
'''Gakkōrei''' (学校令) = A series of five (or four) [[Imperial ordinances]] (standalone edicts) promulgated in [[:en:Japan|Japan]] between [[March 2]] and [[:en:April 10|April 10]], [[:en:1886|1886]] (Meiji 19), regulating the classifications of primary, secondary, and higher educational institutions. These include the '''[[Teikoku Daigakurei]]''', '''[[Shihan Gakkōrei]]''', '''[[Shōgakkōrei]]''', and '''[[Chūgakkōrei]]'''. = A collective term for standalone edicts regulating different types of schools, promulgated from 1886 (Meiji 19) to the enactment of the [[School Education Act]] in [[:en:1947|1947]] (Shōwa 22), as discussed in this article.
----
'''Gakkōrei''' (学校令) refers to a series of [[Imperial ordinances]] established in [[:en:Japan|Japan]] from the [[Meiji period]] onward, replacing the [[Kyōikurei]] and remaining in effect until the enactment of the [[School Education Act]] following [[World War II]]. These ordinances collectively regulated the classifications of schools within the "[[old education system of Japan|old system]]" and the framework of public education. They are sometimes referred to as "'''Shogakkōrei''' (諸学校令)" to emphasize their collective nature.
== Overview == The initial "Gakkōrei" refers to five [[Imperial ordinances]] promulgated between March 2 and April 10, [[:en:1886|1886]]: the [[Teikoku Daigakurei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 3, March 2, Meiji 19), the [[Shihan Gakkōrei]] (Ordinance No. 13, April 10, Meiji 19), the [[Shōgakkōrei]] (Ordinance No. 14, April 10, Meiji 19), the [[Chūgakkōrei]] (Ordinance No. 15, April 10, Meiji 19), and the [[Shogakkō Tsūsoku]] (General School Ordinance, Ordinance No. 16, April 10, Meiji 19). These constitute the "'''narrow definition of Gakkōrei'''" and replaced the [[Kyōikurei=Third Kyōikurei|Third Kyōikurei]].
In its "'''broader definition'''," Gakkōrei refers to the collective term for standalone ordinances that regulated specific school types promulgated sporadically until the [[School Education Act]] of 1947. Unlike the [[Gakusei]] and [[Kyōikurei]] (single comprehensive edicts), the Gakkōrei was not a single legal document but a collective designation for multiple ordinances. To emphasize this distinction, it is sometimes referred to as "Shogakkōrei."
== Development == === Transition from Gakusei and Kyōikurei === Initially, public education in [[modern Japan]] was governed by the [[Gakusei]], established in [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 5), and later replaced by the [[Kyōikurei]] in [[:en:1879|1879]]. These edicts comprehensively regulated various types of schools. However, the rapid expansion of the educational system in the early [[Meiji period]] necessitated significant reforms in the [[:en:1880s|1880s]]. The Kyōikurei underwent complete revisions twice, in [[:en:1880|1880]] and [[:en:1885|1885]], before being replaced by the Gakkōrei series in [[:en:1886|1886]].
=== Mori Arinori and the Creation of Gakkōrei === [[File:Arinori Mori 2.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Mori Arinori, the first [[Minister of Education]], who played a key role in establishing the Gakkōrei.]] [[:en:Mori Arinori|Mori Arinori]], Japan's first [[Minister of Education]], initiated the creation of Gakkōrei during his tenure. Mori believed that education reform should prioritize national stability and align with state objectives. Emphasizing teacher training, he advocated for the establishment of separate ordinances ("individual regulations") to avoid frequent large-scale revisions.
Consequently, between March and April of [[:en:1886|1886]], five standalone edicts were promulgated: the [[Teikoku Daigakurei]], [[Shihan Gakkōrei]], [[Shōgakkōrei]], [[Chūgakkōrei]], and [[Shogakkō Tsūsoku]]. These ordinances collectively replaced the [[Kyōikurei]] and established the foundation of Japan's [[public education system]] under state control. Mori planned to enact additional ordinances, but his assassination in [[:en:1889|1889]] left his vision incomplete.
=== Subsequent Revisions and Additions === The initial ordinances underwent revisions to adapt to Japan's changing educational needs. These revisions clarified the roles of local governments, redefined primary and secondary education, and expanded higher education. Notable reforms included the [[High School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1894|1894]], the [[Private School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1899|1899]], and the [[Specialized School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1903|1903]]. By the [[Taishō period]], the educational system had become highly structured, culminating in the [[University Ordinance]] of [[:en:1918|1918]], which allowed the establishment of non-imperial [[universities]].
=== Wartime Education and Postwar Reforms === During the [[Shōwa period]], Japan's education system was heavily influenced by wartime demands. The [[Youth School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1935|1935]] and the [[Secondary School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1943|1943]] reflected efforts to mobilize the population. Following Japan's defeat in [[World War II]], the [[:en:Allied Occupation of Japan|Allied Occupation of Japan]] mandated educational reforms, leading to the enactment of the [[School Education Act]] in [[:en:1947|1947]], which replaced the old system with a unified modern structure.
== Related Ordinances (Narrow Definition) == === Imperial University Ordinance === {{main|Teikoku Daigakurei}} Established in [[:en:1886|1886]], the [[Teikoku Daigakurei]] defined the structure of [[:en:Imperial Universities|Imperial Universities]]. Subsequent revisions led to its renaming as the [[National Comprehensive University Ordinance]] in [[:en:1947|1947]].
=== Teacher Training Ordinance === {{main|Shihan Gakkōrei|Shihan Kyōikurei}} The [[Shihan Gakkōrei]] regulated [[normal schools]] and was later replaced by the [[Shihan Kyōikurei]] in [[:en:1897|1897]] to enhance teacher training.
=== Primary School Ordinance === {{main|Shōgakkōrei|Kokumin Gakkōrei}} The [[Shōgakkōrei]] established the structure of [[primary education]], later replaced by the [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]] in [[:en:1941|1941]].
=== Middle School Ordinance === {{main|Chūgakkōrei|Chūtō Gakkōrei}} The [[Chūgakkōrei]] defined [[middle schools]] and evolved into the [[Chūtō Gakkōrei]] in [[:en:1943|1943]].
=== General School Ordinance === {{main|Shogakkō Tsūsoku}} The [[Shogakkō Tsūsoku]] regulated school facilities, including libraries and teacher qualifications. It was repealed in [[:en:1900|1900]].
== Broader Scope of Gakkōrei == === Specialized School Ordinance (1903) {{main|Specialized School Ordinance}} === University Ordinance (1918) {{main|University Ordinance}}
== References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group=Notes}}
=== Citations === {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AD%A6%E6%A0%A1%E4%BB%A4-464258 Gakkōrei - Kotobank] * [https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317609.htm "Mori Arinori and the Gakkōrei" - Ministry of Education] * [https://www.archives.go.jp/ayumi/kobetsu/m19_1886_01.html "Public Documents on the Gakkōrei" - Japan National Archives]

These constitute the "'''narrow definition of Gakkōrei'''" and replaced the [[Kyōikurei=Third Kyōikurei|Third Kyōikurei]].

Unlike the [[Gakusei]] and [[Kyōikurei]] (single comprehensive edicts), the Gakkōrei was not a single legal document but a collective designation for multiple ordinances. To emphasize this distinction, it is sometimes referred to as "Shogakkōrei."
== Development == === Transition from Gakusei and Kyōikurei === Initially, public education in [[modern Japan]] was governed by the [[Gakusei]], established in [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 5), and later replaced by the [[Kyōikurei]] in [[:en:1879|1879]]. These edicts comprehensively regulated various types of schools. However, the rapid expansion of the educational system in the early [[Meiji period]] necessitated significant reforms in the [[:en:1880s|1880s]]. The Kyōikurei underwent complete revisions twice, in [[:en:1880|1880]] and [[:en:1885|1885]], before being replaced by the Gakkōrei series in [[:en:1886|1886]].
=== Mori Arinori and the Creation of Gakkōrei === [[File:Arinori Mori 2.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Mori Arinori, the first [[Minister of Education]], who played a key role in establishing the Gakkōrei.]] [[:en:Mori Arinori|Mori Arinori]], Japan's first [[Minister of Education]], initiated the creation of Gakkōrei during his tenure. Mori believed that education reform should prioritize national stability and align with state objectives. Emphasizing teacher training, he advocated for the establishment of separate ordinances ("individual regulations") to avoid frequent large-scale revisions.
Consequently, between March and April of [[:en:1886|1886]], five standalone edicts were promulgated: the [[Teikoku Daigakurei]], [[Shihan Gakkōrei]], [[Shōgakkōrei]], [[Chūgakkōrei]], and [[Shogakkō Tsūsoku]]. These ordinances collectively replaced the [[Kyōikurei]] and established the foundation of Japan's [[public education system]] under state control. Mori planned to enact additional ordinances, but his assassination in [[:en:1889|1889]] left his vision incomplete.
=== Subsequent Revisions and Additions === The initial ordinances underwent revisions to adapt to Japan's changing educational needs. These revisions clarified the roles of local governments, redefined primary and secondary education, and expanded higher education. Notable reforms included the [[High School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1894|1894]], the [[Private School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1899|1899]], and the [[Specialized School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1903|1903]]. By the [[Taishō period]], the educational system had become highly structured, culminating in the [[University Ordinance]] of [[:en:1918|1918]], which allowed the establishment of non-imperial [[universities]].
=== Wartime Education and Postwar Reforms === During the [[Shōwa period]], Japan's education system was heavily influenced by wartime demands. The [[Youth School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1935|1935]] and the [[Secondary School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1943|1943]] reflected efforts to mobilize the population. Following Japan's defeat in [[World War II]], the [[:en:Allied Occupation of Japan|Allied Occupation of Japan]] mandated educational reforms, leading to the enactment of the [[School Education Act]] in [[:en:1947|1947]], which replaced the old system with a unified modern structure.
== Related Ordinances (Narrow Definition) == === Imperial University Ordinance === {{main|Teikoku Daigakurei}} Established in [[:en:1886|1886]], the [[Teikoku Daigakurei]] defined the structure of [[:en:Imperial Universities|Imperial Universities]]. Subsequent revisions led to its renaming as the [[National Comprehensive University Ordinance]] in [[:en:1947|1947]].
=== Teacher Training Ordinance === {{main|Shihan Gakkōrei|Shihan Kyōikurei}} The [[Shihan Gakkōrei]] regulated [[normal schools]] and was later replaced by the [[Shihan Kyōikurei]] in [[:en:1897|1897]] to enhance teacher training.
=== Primary School Ordinance === {{main|Shōgakkōrei|Kokumin Gakkōrei}} The [[Shōgakkōrei]] established the structure of [[primary education]], later replaced by the [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]] in [[:en:1941|1941]].
=== Middle School Ordinance === {{main|Chūgakkōrei|Chūtō Gakkōrei}} The [[Chūgakkōrei]] defined [[middle schools]] and evolved into the [[Chūtō Gakkōrei]] in [[:en:1943|1943]].
=== General School Ordinance === {{main|Shogakkō Tsūsoku}} The [[Shogakkō Tsūsoku]] regulated school facilities, including libraries and teacher qualifications. It was repealed in [[:en:1900|1900]].
== Broader Scope of Gakkōrei == === Specialized School Ordinance (1903) {{main|Specialized School Ordinance}} === University Ordinance (1918) {{main|University Ordinance}}
== References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group=Notes}}
=== Citations === {{Reflist}}

'''Gakkōrei''' (学校令) = A series of five (or four) [[Imperial ordinances]] (standalone edicts) promulgated in [[:en:Japan|Japan]] between [[March 2]] and [[:en:April 10|April 10]], [[:en:1886|1886]] (Meiji 19), regulating the classifications of primary, secondary, and higher educational institutions. These include the '''[[Teikoku Daigakurei]]''', '''[[Shihan Gakkōrei]]''', '''[[Shōgakkōrei]]''', and '''[[Chūgakkōrei]]'''. = A collective term for standalone edicts regulating different types of schools, promulgated from 1886 (Meiji 19) to the enactment of the [[School Education Act]] in [[:en:1947|1947]] (Shōwa 22), as discussed in this article.
----
'''Gakkōrei''' (学校令) refers to a series of [[Imperial ordinances]] established in [[:en:Japan|Japan]] from the [[Meiji period]] onward, replacing the [[Kyōikurei]] and remaining in effect until the enactment of the [[School Education Act]] following [[World War II]]. These ordinances collectively regulated the classifications of schools within the "[[old education system of Japan|old system]]" and the framework of public education. They are sometimes referred to as "'''Shogakkōrei''' (諸学校令)" to emphasize their collective nature.
== Overview == The initial "Gakkōrei" refers to five [[Imperial ordinances]] promulgated between March 2 and April 10, [[:en:1886|1886]]: the [[Teikoku Daigakurei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 3, March 2, Meiji 19), the [[Shihan Gakkōrei]] (Ordinance No. 13, April 10, Meiji 19), the [[Shōgakkōrei]] (Ordinance No. 14, April 10, Meiji 19), the [[Chūgakkōrei]] (Ordinance No. 15, April 10, Meiji 19), and the [[Shogakkō Tsūsoku]] (General School Ordinance, Ordinance No. 16, April 10, Meiji 19). These constitute the "'''narrow definition of Gakkōrei'''" and replaced the [[Kyōikurei=Third Kyōikurei|Third Kyōikurei]].
In its "'''broader definition'''," Gakkōrei refers to the collective term for standalone ordinances that regulated specific school types promulgated sporadically until the [[School Education Act]] of 1947. Unlike the [[Gakusei]] and [[Kyōikurei]] (single comprehensive edicts), the Gakkōrei was not a single legal document but a collective designation for multiple ordinances. To emphasize this distinction, it is sometimes referred to as "Shogakkōrei."
== Development == === Transition from Gakusei and Kyōikurei === Initially, public education in [[modern Japan]] was governed by the [[Gakusei]], established in [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 5), and later replaced by the [[Kyōikurei]] in [[:en:1879|1879]]. These edicts comprehensively regulated various types of schools. However, the rapid expansion of the educational system in the early [[Meiji period]] necessitated significant reforms in the [[:en:1880s|1880s]]. The Kyōikurei underwent complete revisions twice, in [[:en:1880|1880]] and [[:en:1885|1885]], before being replaced by the Gakkōrei series in [[:en:1886|1886]].
=== Mori Arinori and the Creation of Gakkōrei === [[File:Arinori Mori 2.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Mori Arinori, the first [[Minister of Education]], who played a key role in establishing the Gakkōrei.]] [[:en:Mori Arinori|Mori Arinori]], Japan's first [[Minister of Education]], initiated the creation of Gakkōrei during his tenure. Mori believed that education reform should prioritize national stability and align with state objectives. Emphasizing teacher training, he advocated for the establishment of separate ordinances ("individual regulations") to avoid frequent large-scale revisions.
Consequently, between March and April of [[:en:1886|1886]], five standalone edicts were promulgated: the [[Teikoku Daigakurei]], [[Shihan Gakkōrei]], [[Shōgakkōrei]], [[Chūgakkōrei]], and [[Shogakkō Tsūsoku]]. These ordinances collectively replaced the [[Kyōikurei]] and established the foundation of Japan's [[public education system]] under state control. Mori planned to enact additional ordinances, but his assassination in [[:en:1889|1889]] left his vision incomplete.
=== Subsequent Revisions and Additions === The initial ordinances underwent revisions to adapt to Japan's changing educational needs. These revisions clarified the roles of local governments, redefined primary and secondary education, and expanded higher education. Notable reforms included the [[High School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1894|1894]], the [[Private School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1899|1899]], and the [[Specialized School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1903|1903]]. By the [[Taishō period]], the educational system had become highly structured, culminating in the [[University Ordinance]] of [[:en:1918|1918]], which allowed the establishment of non-imperial [[universities]].
=== Wartime Education and Postwar Reforms === During the [[Shōwa period]], Japan's education system was heavily influenced by wartime demands. The [[Youth School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1935|1935]] and the [[Secondary School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1943|1943]] reflected efforts to mobilize the population. Following Japan's defeat in [[World War II]], the [[:en:Allied Occupation of Japan|Allied Occupation of Japan]] mandated educational reforms, leading to the enactment of the [[School Education Act]] in [[:en:1947|1947]], which replaced the old system with a unified modern structure.
== Related Ordinances (Narrow Definition) == === Imperial University Ordinance === {{main|Teikoku Daigakurei}} Established in [[:en:1886|1886]], the [[Teikoku Daigakurei]] defined the structure of [[:en:Imperial Universities|Imperial Universities]]. Subsequent revisions led to its renaming as the [[National Comprehensive University Ordinance]] in [[:en:1947|1947]].
=== Teacher Training Ordinance === {{main|Shihan Gakkōrei|Shihan Kyōikurei}} The [[Shihan Gakkōrei]] regulated [[normal schools]] and was later replaced by the [[Shihan Kyōikurei]] in [[:en:1897|1897]] to enhance teacher training.

=== General School Ordinance === {{main|Shogakkō Tsūsoku}} The [[Shogakkō Tsūsoku]] regulated school facilities, including libraries and teacher qualifications. It was repealed in [[:en:1900|1900]].

[[Category:1886 in law]] [[Category:Laws Abolished in 1947]] [[Category:19th Century Education in Japan]] [[Category:20th Century Education in Japan]] [[Category:Abolished Imperial Decrees of Japan]] [[Category:Education in the Empire of Japan]] [[Category:Laws for Former Educational Institutions of Japan]] [[Category:Former Educational Laws of Japan]] [[Category:明治時代の教育]] [[Category:Mori Arinori]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis-->

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
'''Gakkōrei''' (学校令) = A series of five (or four) [[Imperial ordinances]] (standalone edicts) promulgated in [[:en:Japan|Japan]] between [[:en:March 2|March 2]] and [[:en:April 10|April 10]], [[:en:1886|1886]] (Meiji 19), regulating the classifications of primary, secondary, and higher educational institutions. These include the '''[[Teikoku Daigakurei]]''', '''[[Shihan Gakkōrei]]''', '''[[Shōgakkōrei]]''', and '''[[Chūgakkōrei]]'''. = A collective term for standalone edicts regulating different types of schools, promulgated from 1886 (Meiji 19) to the enactment of the [[School Education Act]] in [[:en:1947|1947]] (Shōwa 22), as discussed in this article.
----
'''Gakkōrei''' (学校令) refers to a series of [[Imperial ordinances]] established in [[:en:Japan|Japan]] from the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] onward, replacing the [[Kyōikurei]] and remaining in effect until the enactment of the [[School Education Act]] following [[World War II]]. These ordinances collectively regulated the classifications of schools within the "[[old education system of Japan|old system]]" and the framework of public education. They are sometimes referred to as "'''Shogakkōrei''' (諸学校令)" to emphasize their collective nature.
== Overview == The initial "Gakkōrei" refers to five [[Imperial ordinances]] promulgated between March 2 and April 10, [[:en:1886|1886]]: the [[Teikoku Daigakurei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 3, March 2, Meiji 19), the [[Shihan Gakkōrei]] (Ordinance No. 13, April 10, Meiji 19), the [[Shōgakkōrei]] (Ordinance No. 14, April 10, Meiji 19), the [[Chūgakkōrei]] (Ordinance No. 15, April 10, Meiji 19), and the [[Shogakkō Tsūsoku]] (General School Ordinance, Ordinance No. 16, April 10, Meiji 19). These constitute the "'''narrow definition of Gakkōrei'''" and replaced the [[Kyōikurei=Third Kyōikurei|Third Kyōikurei]].
In its "'''broader definition'''," Gakkōrei refers to the collective term for standalone ordinances that regulated specific school types promulgated sporadically until the [[School Education Act]] of 1947. Unlike the [[Gakusei]] and [[Kyōikurei]] (single comprehensive edicts), the Gakkōrei was not a single legal document but a collective designation for multiple ordinances. To emphasize this distinction, it is sometimes referred to as "Shogakkōrei."
== Development == === Transition from Gakusei and Kyōikurei === Initially, public education in [[modern Japan]] was governed by the [[Gakusei]], established in [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 5), and later replaced by the [[Kyōikurei]] in [[:en:1879|1879]]. These edicts comprehensively regulated various types of schools. However, the rapid expansion of the educational system in the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] necessitated significant reforms in the [[:en:1880s|1880s]]. The Kyōikurei underwent complete revisions twice, in [[:en:1880|1880]] and [[:en:1885|1885]], before being replaced by the Gakkōrei series in [[:en:1886|1886]].
=== Mori Arinori and the Creation of Gakkōrei === [[File:Arinori Mori 2.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Mori Arinori, the first [[Minister of Education]], who played a key role in establishing the Gakkōrei.]] [[:en:Mori Arinori|Mori Arinori]], Japan's first [[Minister of Education]], initiated the creation of Gakkōrei during his tenure. Mori believed that education reform should prioritize national stability and align with state objectives. Emphasizing teacher training, he advocated for the establishment of separate ordinances ("individual regulations") to avoid frequent large-scale revisions.
Consequently, between March and April of [[:en:1886|1886]], five standalone edicts were promulgated: the [[Teikoku Daigakurei]], [[Shihan Gakkōrei]], [[Shōgakkōrei]], [[Chūgakkōrei]], and [[Shogakkō Tsūsoku]]. These ordinances collectively replaced the [[Kyōikurei]] and established the foundation of Japan's [[public education system]] under state control. Mori planned to enact additional ordinances, but his assassination in [[:en:1889|1889]] left his vision incomplete.
=== Subsequent Revisions and Additions === The initial ordinances underwent revisions to adapt to Japan's changing educational needs. These revisions clarified the roles of local governments, redefined primary and secondary education, and expanded higher education. Notable reforms included the [[High School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1894|1894]], the [[Private School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1899|1899]], and the [[Specialized School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1903|1903]]. By the [[Taishō period]], the educational system had become highly structured, culminating in the [[University Ordinance]] of [[:en:1918|1918]], which allowed the establishment of non-imperial [[universities]].
=== Wartime Education and Postwar Reforms === During the [[Shōwa period]], Japan's education system was heavily influenced by wartime demands. The [[Youth School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1935|1935]] and the [[Secondary School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1943|1943]] reflected efforts to mobilize the population. Following Japan's defeat in [[World War II]], the [[:en:Allied Occupation of Japan|Allied Occupation of Japan]] mandated educational reforms, leading to the enactment of the [[School Education Act]] in [[:en:1947|1947]], which replaced the old system with a unified modern structure.
== Related Ordinances (Narrow Definition) == === Imperial University Ordinance === {{main|Teikoku Daigakurei}} Established in [[:en:1886|1886]], the [[Teikoku Daigakurei]] defined the structure of [[:en:Imperial Universities|Imperial Universities]]. Subsequent revisions led to its renaming as the [[National Comprehensive University Ordinance]] in [[:en:1947|1947]].
=== Teacher Training Ordinance === {{main|Shihan Gakkōrei|Shihan Kyōikurei}} The [[Shihan Gakkōrei]] regulated [[normal schools]] and was later replaced by the [[Shihan Kyōikurei]] in [[:en:1897|1897]] to enhance teacher training.
=== Primary School Ordinance === {{main|Shōgakkōrei|Kokumin Gakkōrei}} The [[Shōgakkōrei]] established the structure of [[primary education]], later replaced by the [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]] in [[:en:1941|1941]].
=== Middle School Ordinance === {{main|Chūgakkōrei|Chūtō Gakkōrei}} The [[Chūgakkōrei]] defined [[middle schools]] and evolved into the [[Chūtō Gakkōrei]] in [[:en:1943|1943]].
=== General School Ordinance === {{main|Shogakkō Tsūsoku}} The [[Shogakkō Tsūsoku]] regulated school facilities, including libraries and teacher qualifications. It was repealed in [[:en:1900|1900]].
== Broader Scope of Gakkōrei == === Specialized School Ordinance (1903) {{main|Specialized School Ordinance}} === University Ordinance (1918) {{main|University Ordinance}}
== References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group=Notes}}
=== Citations === {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AD%A6%E6%A0%A1%E4%BB%A4-464258 Gakkōrei - Kotobank] * [https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317609.htm "Mori Arinori and the Gakkōrei" - Ministry of Education] * [https://www.archives.go.jp/ayumi/kobetsu/m19_1886_01.html "Public Documents on the Gakkōrei" - Japan National Archives]

=== Mori Arinori and the Creation of Gakkōrei === [[File:Arinori Mori 2.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Mori Arinori, the first [[:en:Minister of Education|Minister of Education]], who played a key role in establishing the Gakkōrei.]] [[:en:Mori Arinori|Mori Arinori]], Japan's first [[:en:Minister of Education|Minister of Education]], initiated the creation of Gakkōrei during his tenure. Mori believed that education reform should prioritize national stability and align with state objectives. Emphasizing teacher training, he advocated for the establishment of separate ordinances ("individual regulations") to avoid frequent large-scale revisions.
Consequently, between March and April of [[:en:1886|1886]], five standalone edicts were promulgated: the [[Teikoku Daigakurei]], [[Shihan Gakkōrei]], [[Shōgakkōrei]], [[Chūgakkōrei]], and [[Shogakkō Tsūsoku]]. These ordinances collectively replaced the [[Kyōikurei]] and established the foundation of Japan's [[public education system]] under state control. Mori planned to enact additional ordinances, but his assassination in [[:en:1889|1889]] left his vision incomplete.
=== Subsequent Revisions and Additions === The initial ordinances underwent revisions to adapt to Japan's changing educational needs. These revisions clarified the roles of local governments, redefined primary and secondary education, and expanded higher education. Notable reforms included the [[High School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1894|1894]], the [[Private School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1899|1899]], and the [[Specialized School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1903|1903]]. By the [[Taishō period]], the educational system had become highly structured, culminating in the [[University Ordinance]] of [[:en:1918|1918]], which allowed the establishment of non-imperial [[universities]].
=== Wartime Education and Postwar Reforms === During the [[Shōwa period]], Japan's education system was heavily influenced by wartime demands. The [[Youth School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1935|1935]] and the [[Secondary School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1943|1943]] reflected efforts to mobilize the population. Following Japan's defeat in [[World War II]], the [[:en:Allied Occupation of Japan|Allied Occupation of Japan]] mandated educational reforms, leading to the enactment of the [[School Education Act]] in [[:en:1947|1947]], which replaced the old system with a unified modern structure.
== Related Ordinances (Narrow Definition) == === Imperial University Ordinance === {{main|Teikoku Daigakurei}} Established in [[:en:1886|1886]], the [[Teikoku Daigakurei]] defined the structure of [[:en:Imperial Universities|Imperial Universities]]. Subsequent revisions led to its renaming as the [[National Comprehensive University Ordinance]] in [[:en:1947|1947]].
=== Teacher Training Ordinance === {{main|Shihan Gakkōrei|Shihan Kyōikurei}} The [[Shihan Gakkōrei]] regulated [[normal schools]] and was later replaced by the [[Shihan Kyōikurei]] in [[:en:1897|1897]] to enhance teacher training.
=== Primary School Ordinance === {{main|Shōgakkōrei|Kokumin Gakkōrei}} The [[Shōgakkōrei]] established the structure of [[primary education]], later replaced by the [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]] in [[:en:1941|1941]].
=== Middle School Ordinance === {{main|Chūgakkōrei|Chūtō Gakkōrei}} The [[Chūgakkōrei]] defined [[:en:middle schools|middle schools]] and evolved into the [[Chūtō Gakkōrei]] in [[:en:1943|1943]].
=== General School Ordinance === {{main|Shogakkō Tsūsoku}} The [[Shogakkō Tsūsoku]] regulated school facilities, including libraries and teacher qualifications. It was repealed in [[:en:1900|1900]].
== Broader Scope of Gakkōrei == === Specialized School Ordinance (1903) {{main|Specialized School Ordinance}} === University Ordinance (1918) {{main|University Ordinance}}

revision.text:
'''Gakkōrei''' (学校令) = A series of five (or four) [[Imperial ordinances]] (standalone edicts) promulgated in [[:en:Japan|Japan]] between [[:en:March 2|March 2]] and [[:en:April 10|April 10]], [[:en:1886|1886]] (Meiji 19), regulating the classifications of primary, secondary, and higher educational institutions. These include the '''[[Teikoku Daigakurei]]''', '''[[Shihan Gakkōrei]]''', '''[[Shōgakkōrei]]''', and '''[[Chūgakkōrei]]'''. = A collective term for standalone edicts regulating different types of schools, promulgated from 1886 (Meiji 19) to the enactment of the [[School Education Act]] in [[:en:1947|1947]] (Shōwa 22), as discussed in this article.
----
'''Gakkōrei''' (学校令) refers to a series of [[Imperial ordinances]] established in [[:en:Japan|Japan]] from the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] onward, replacing the [[Kyōikurei]] and remaining in effect until the enactment of the [[School Education Act]] following [[World War II]]. These ordinances collectively regulated the classifications of schools within the "[[old education system of Japan|old system]]" and the framework of public education. They are sometimes referred to as "'''Shogakkōrei''' (諸学校令)" to emphasize their collective nature.
== Overview == The initial "Gakkōrei" refers to five [[Imperial ordinances]] promulgated between March 2 and April 10, [[:en:1886|1886]]: the [[Teikoku Daigakurei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 3, March 2, Meiji 19), the [[Shihan Gakkōrei]] (Ordinance No. 13, April 10, Meiji 19), the [[Shōgakkōrei]] (Ordinance No. 14, April 10, Meiji 19), the [[Chūgakkōrei]] (Ordinance No. 15, April 10, Meiji 19), and the [[Shogakkō Tsūsoku]] (General School Ordinance, Ordinance No. 16, April 10, Meiji 19). These constitute the "'''narrow definition of Gakkōrei'''" and replaced the [[Kyōikurei=Third Kyōikurei|Third Kyōikurei]].
In its "'''broader definition'''," Gakkōrei refers to the collective term for standalone ordinances that regulated specific school types promulgated sporadically until the [[School Education Act]] of 1947. Unlike the [[Gakusei]] and [[Kyōikurei]] (single comprehensive edicts), the Gakkōrei was not a single legal document but a collective designation for multiple ordinances. To emphasize this distinction, it is sometimes referred to as "Shogakkōrei."
== Development == === Transition from Gakusei and Kyōikurei === Initially, public education in [[:en:Modern Japan|Modern Japan]] was governed by the [[Gakusei]], established in [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 5), and later replaced by the [[Kyōikurei]] in [[:en:1879|1879]]. These edicts comprehensively regulated various types of schools. However, the rapid expansion of the educational system in the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] necessitated significant reforms in the [[:en:1880s|1880s]]. The Kyōikurei underwent complete revisions twice, in [[:en:1880|1880]] and [[:en:1885|1885]], before being replaced by the Gakkōrei series in [[:en:1886|1886]].
=== Mori Arinori and the Creation of Gakkōrei === [[File:Arinori Mori 2.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Mori Arinori, the first [[:en:Minister of Education|Minister of Education]], who played a key role in establishing the Gakkōrei.]] [[:en:Mori Arinori|Mori Arinori]], Japan's first [[:en:Minister of Education|Minister of Education]], initiated the creation of Gakkōrei during his tenure. Mori believed that education reform should prioritize national stability and align with state objectives. Emphasizing teacher training, he advocated for the establishment of separate ordinances ("individual regulations") to avoid frequent large-scale revisions.
Consequently, between March and April of [[:en:1886|1886]], five standalone edicts were promulgated: the [[Teikoku Daigakurei]], [[Shihan Gakkōrei]], [[Shōgakkōrei]], [[Chūgakkōrei]], and [[Shogakkō Tsūsoku]]. These ordinances collectively replaced the [[Kyōikurei]] and established the foundation of Japan's [[public education system]] under state control. Mori planned to enact additional ordinances, but his assassination in [[:en:1889|1889]] left his vision incomplete.
=== Subsequent Revisions and Additions === The initial ordinances underwent revisions to adapt to Japan's changing educational needs. These revisions clarified the roles of local governments, redefined primary and secondary education, and expanded higher education. Notable reforms included the [[High School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1894|1894]], the [[Private School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1899|1899]], and the [[Specialized School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1903|1903]]. By the [[Taishō period]], the educational system had become highly structured, culminating in the [[University Ordinance]] of [[:en:1918|1918]], which allowed the establishment of non-imperial [[universities]].
=== Wartime Education and Postwar Reforms === During the [[Shōwa period]], Japan's education system was heavily influenced by wartime demands. The [[Youth School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1935|1935]] and the [[Secondary School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1943|1943]] reflected efforts to mobilize the population. Following Japan's defeat in [[World War II]], the [[:en:Allied Occupation of Japan|Allied Occupation of Japan]] mandated educational reforms, leading to the enactment of the [[School Education Act]] in [[:en:1947|1947]], which replaced the old system with a unified modern structure.
== Related Ordinances (Narrow Definition) == === Imperial University Ordinance === {{main|Teikoku Daigakurei}} Established in [[:en:1886|1886]], the [[Teikoku Daigakurei]] defined the structure of [[:en:Imperial Universities|Imperial Universities]]. Subsequent revisions led to its renaming as the [[National Comprehensive University Ordinance]] in [[:en:1947|1947]].
=== Teacher Training Ordinance === {{main|Shihan Gakkōrei|Shihan Kyōikurei}} The [[Shihan Gakkōrei]] regulated [[:en:normal schools|normal schools]] and was later replaced by the [[Shihan Kyōikurei]] in [[:en:1897|1897]] to enhance teacher training.
=== Primary School Ordinance === {{main|Shōgakkōrei|Kokumin Gakkōrei}} The [[Shōgakkōrei]] established the structure of [[primary education]], later replaced by the [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]] in [[:en:1941|1941]].
=== Middle School Ordinance === {{main|Chūgakkōrei|Chūtō Gakkōrei}} The [[Chūgakkōrei]] defined [[:en:middle schools|middle schools]] and evolved into the [[Chūtō Gakkōrei]] in [[:en:1943|1943]].
=== General School Ordinance === {{main|Shogakkō Tsūsoku}} The [[Shogakkō Tsūsoku]] regulated school facilities, including libraries and teacher qualifications. It was repealed in [[:en:1900|1900]].
== Broader Scope of Gakkōrei == === Specialized School Ordinance (1903) {{main|Specialized School Ordinance}} === University Ordinance (1918) {{main|University Ordinance}}

[[Category:1886 in law]] [[Category:Laws Abolished in 1947]] [[Category:19th Century Education in Japan]] [[Category:20th Century Education in Japan]] [[Category:Abolished Imperial Decrees of Japan]] [[Category:Education in the Empire of Japan]] [[Category:Laws for Former Educational Institutions of Japan]] [[Category:Former Educational Laws of Japan]] [[Category:明治時代の教育]] [[Category:Mori Arinori]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis-->

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
'''Gakkōrei''' (学校令) = A series of five (or four) [[Imperial ordinances]] (standalone edicts) promulgated in [[:en:Japan|Japan]] between [[:en:March 2|March 2]] and [[:en:April 10|April 10]], [[:en:1886|1886]] (Meiji 19), regulating the classifications of primary, secondary, and higher educational institutions. These include the '''[[Teikoku Daigakurei]]''', '''[[Shihan Gakkōrei]]''', '''[[Shōgakkōrei]]''', and '''[[Chūgakkōrei]]'''. = A collective term for standalone edicts regulating different types of schools, promulgated from 1886 (Meiji 19) to the enactment of the [[School Education Act]] in [[:en:1947|1947]] (Shōwa 22), as discussed in this article.
----
'''Gakkōrei''' (学校令) refers to a series of [[Imperial ordinances]] established in [[:en:Japan|Japan]] from the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] onward, replacing the [[Kyōikurei]] and remaining in effect until the enactment of the [[School Education Act]] following [[World War II]]. These ordinances collectively regulated the classifications of schools within the "[[old education system of Japan|old system]]" and the framework of public education. They are sometimes referred to as "'''Shogakkōrei''' (諸学校令)" to emphasize their collective nature.
== Overview == The initial "Gakkōrei" refers to five [[Imperial ordinances]] promulgated between March 2 and April 10, [[:en:1886|1886]]: the [[Teikoku Daigakurei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 3, March 2, Meiji 19), the [[Shihan Gakkōrei]] (Ordinance No. 13, April 10, Meiji 19), the [[Shōgakkōrei]] (Ordinance No. 14, April 10, Meiji 19), the [[Chūgakkōrei]] (Ordinance No. 15, April 10, Meiji 19), and the [[Shogakkō Tsūsoku]] (General School Ordinance, Ordinance No. 16, April 10, Meiji 19). These constitute the "'''narrow definition of Gakkōrei'''" and replaced the [[Kyōikurei=Third Kyōikurei|Third Kyōikurei]].
In its "'''broader definition'''," Gakkōrei refers to the collective term for standalone ordinances that regulated specific school types promulgated sporadically until the [[School Education Act]] of 1947. Unlike the [[Gakusei]] and [[Kyōikurei]] (single comprehensive edicts), the Gakkōrei was not a single legal document but a collective designation for multiple ordinances. To emphasize this distinction, it is sometimes referred to as "Shogakkōrei."
== Development == === Transition from Gakusei and Kyōikurei === Initially, public education in [[:en:Modern Japan|Modern Japan]] was governed by the [[Gakusei]], established in [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 5), and later replaced by the [[Kyōikurei]] in [[:en:1879|1879]]. These edicts comprehensively regulated various types of schools. However, the rapid expansion of the educational system in the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] necessitated significant reforms in the [[:en:1880s|1880s]]. The Kyōikurei underwent complete revisions twice, in [[:en:1880|1880]] and [[:en:1885|1885]], before being replaced by the Gakkōrei series in [[:en:1886|1886]].
=== Mori Arinori and the Creation of Gakkōrei === [[File:Arinori Mori 2.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Mori Arinori, the first [[:en:Minister of Education|Minister of Education]], who played a key role in establishing the Gakkōrei.]] [[:en:Mori Arinori|Mori Arinori]], Japan's first [[:en:Minister of Education|Minister of Education]], initiated the creation of Gakkōrei during his tenure. Mori believed that education reform should prioritize national stability and align with state objectives. Emphasizing teacher training, he advocated for the establishment of separate ordinances ("individual regulations") to avoid frequent large-scale revisions.
Consequently, between March and April of [[:en:1886|1886]], five standalone edicts were promulgated: the [[Teikoku Daigakurei]], [[Shihan Gakkōrei]], [[Shōgakkōrei]], [[Chūgakkōrei]], and [[Shogakkō Tsūsoku]]. These ordinances collectively replaced the [[Kyōikurei]] and established the foundation of Japan's [[public education system]] under state control. Mori planned to enact additional ordinances, but his assassination in [[:en:1889|1889]] left his vision incomplete.
=== Subsequent Revisions and Additions === The initial ordinances underwent revisions to adapt to Japan's changing educational needs. These revisions clarified the roles of local governments, redefined primary and secondary education, and expanded higher education. Notable reforms included the [[High School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1894|1894]], the [[Private School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1899|1899]], and the [[Specialized School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1903|1903]]. By the [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō era]], the educational system had become highly structured, culminating in the [[University Ordinance]] of [[:en:1918|1918]], which allowed the establishment of non-imperial [[:en:universities|universities]].
=== Wartime Education and Postwar Reforms === During the [[Shōwa period]], Japan's education system was heavily influenced by wartime demands. The [[Youth School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1935|1935]] and the [[Secondary School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1943|1943]] reflected efforts to mobilize the population. Following Japan's defeat in [[World War II]], the [[:en:Allied Occupation of Japan|Allied Occupation of Japan]] mandated educational reforms, leading to the enactment of the [[School Education Act]] in [[:en:1947|1947]], which replaced the old system with a unified modern structure.
== Related Ordinances (Narrow Definition) == === Imperial University Ordinance === {{main|Teikoku Daigakurei}} Established in [[:en:1886|1886]], the [[Teikoku Daigakurei]] defined the structure of [[:en:Imperial Universities|Imperial Universities]]. Subsequent revisions led to its renaming as the [[National Comprehensive University Ordinance]] in [[:en:1947|1947]].
=== Teacher Training Ordinance === {{main|Shihan Gakkōrei|Shihan Kyōikurei}} The [[Shihan Gakkōrei]] regulated [[:en:normal schools|normal schools]] and was later replaced by the [[Shihan Kyōikurei]] in [[:en:1897|1897]] to enhance teacher training.
=== Primary School Ordinance === {{main|Shōgakkōrei|Kokumin Gakkōrei}} The [[Shōgakkōrei]] established the structure of [[:en:primary education|primary education]], later replaced by the [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]] in [[:en:1941|1941]].
=== Middle School Ordinance === {{main|Chūgakkōrei|Chūtō Gakkōrei}} The [[Chūgakkōrei]] defined [[:en:middle schools|middle schools]] and evolved into the [[Chūtō Gakkōrei]] in [[:en:1943|1943]].
=== General School Ordinance === {{main|Shogakkō Tsūsoku}} The [[Shogakkō Tsūsoku]] regulated school facilities, including libraries and teacher qualifications. It was repealed in [[:en:1900|1900]].
== Broader Scope of Gakkōrei == === Specialized School Ordinance (1903) {{main|Specialized School Ordinance}} === University Ordinance (1918) {{main|University Ordinance}}
== References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group=Notes}}
=== Citations === {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%AD%A6%E6%A0%A1%E4%BB%A4-464258 Gakkōrei - Kotobank] * [https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317609.htm "Mori Arinori and the Gakkōrei" - Ministry of Education] * [https://www.archives.go.jp/ayumi/kobetsu/m19_1886_01.html "Public Documents on the Gakkōrei" - Japan National Archives]

These edicts comprehensively regulated various types of schools. However, the rapid expansion of the educational system in the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] necessitated significant reforms in the [[:en:1880s|1880s]]. The Kyōikurei underwent complete revisions twice, in [[:en:1880|1880]] and [[:en:1885|1885]], before being replaced by the Gakkōrei series in [[:en:1886|1886]].
=== Mori Arinori and the Creation of Gakkōrei === [[File:Arinori Mori 2.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Mori Arinori, the first [[:en:Minister of Education|Minister of Education]], who played a key role in establishing the Gakkōrei.]] [[:en:Mori Arinori|Mori Arinori]], Japan's first [[:en:Minister of Education|Minister of Education]], initiated the creation of Gakkōrei during his tenure. Mori believed that education reform should prioritize national stability and align with state objectives. Emphasizing teacher training, he advocated for the establishment of separate ordinances ("individual regulations") to avoid frequent large-scale revisions.
Consequently, between March and April of [[:en:1886|1886]], five standalone edicts were promulgated: the [[Teikoku Daigakurei]], [[Shihan Gakkōrei]], [[Shōgakkōrei]], [[Chūgakkōrei]], and [[Shogakkō Tsūsoku]]. These ordinances collectively replaced the [[Kyōikurei]] and established the foundation of Japan's [[public education system]] under state control. Mori planned to enact additional ordinances, but his assassination in [[:en:1889|1889]] left his vision incomplete.
=== Subsequent Revisions and Additions === The initial ordinances underwent revisions to adapt to Japan's changing educational needs. These revisions clarified the roles of local governments, redefined primary and secondary education, and expanded higher education. Notable reforms included the [[High School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1894|1894]], the [[Private School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1899|1899]], and the [[Specialized School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1903|1903]]. By the [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō era]], the educational system had become highly structured, culminating in the [[University Ordinance]] of [[:en:1918|1918]], which allowed the establishment of non-imperial [[:en:universities|universities]].
=== Wartime Education and Postwar Reforms === During the [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa era]], Japan's education system was heavily influenced by wartime demands. The [[Youth School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1935|1935]] and the [[Secondary School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1943|1943]] reflected efforts to mobilize the population. Following Japan's defeat in [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], the [[:en:Allied Occupation of Japan|Allied Occupation of Japan]] mandated educational reforms, leading to the enactment of the [[School Education Act]] in [[:en:1947|1947]], which replaced the old system with a unified modern structure.
== Related Ordinances (Narrow Definition) == === Imperial University Ordinance === {{main|Teikoku Daigakurei}} Established in [[:en:1886|1886]], the [[Teikoku Daigakurei]] defined the structure of [[:en:Imperial Universities|Imperial Universities]]. Subsequent revisions led to its renaming as the [[National Comprehensive University Ordinance]] in [[:en:1947|1947]].
=== Teacher Training Ordinance === {{main|Shihan Gakkōrei|Shihan Kyōikurei}} The [[Shihan Gakkōrei]] regulated [[:en:normal schools|normal schools]] and was later replaced by the [[Shihan Kyōikurei]] in [[:en:1897|1897]] to enhance teacher training.
=== Primary School Ordinance === {{main|Shōgakkōrei|Kokumin Gakkōrei}} The [[Shōgakkōrei]] established the structure of [[:en:primary education|primary education]], later replaced by the [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]] in [[:en:1941|1941]].
=== Middle School Ordinance === {{main|Chūgakkōrei|Chūtō Gakkōrei}} The [[Chūgakkōrei]] defined [[:en:middle schools|middle schools]] and evolved into the [[Chūtō Gakkōrei]] in [[:en:1943|1943]].
=== General School Ordinance === {{main|Shogakkō Tsūsoku}} The [[Shogakkō Tsūsoku]] regulated school facilities, including libraries and teacher qualifications. It was repealed in [[:en:1900|1900]].
== Broader Scope of Gakkōrei == === Specialized School Ordinance (1903) {{main|Specialized School Ordinance}} === University Ordinance (1918) {{main|University Ordinance}}
== References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group=Notes}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q17224447]] Wikidata name: school order [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:08, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Gakkōrei''' (学校令) = A series of five (or four) [[Imperial ordinances]] (standalone edicts) promulgated in [[:en:Japan|Japan]] between [[:en:March 2|March 2]] and [[:en:April 10|April 10]], [[:en:1886|1886]] (Meiji 19), regulating the classifications of primary, secondary, and higher educational institutions. These include the '''[[Teikoku Daigakurei]]''', '''[[Shihan Gakkōrei]]''', '''[[Shōgakkōrei]]''', and '''[[Chūgakkōrei]]'''. = A collective term for standalone edicts regulating different types of schools, promulgated from 1886 (Meiji 19) to the enactment of the [[School Education Act]] in [[:en:1947|1947]] (Shōwa 22), as discussed in this article.
----
'''Gakkōrei''' (学校令) refers to a series of [[Imperial ordinances]] established in [[:en:Japan|Japan]] from the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] onward, replacing the [[Kyōikurei]] and remaining in effect until the enactment of the [[School Education Act]] following [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]]. These ordinances collectively regulated the classifications of schools within the "[[old education system of Japan|old system]]" and the framework of public education. They are sometimes referred to as "'''Shogakkōrei''' (諸学校令)" to emphasize their collective nature.
== Overview == The initial "Gakkōrei" refers to five [[Imperial ordinances]] promulgated between March 2 and April 10, [[:en:1886|1886]]: the [[Teikoku Daigakurei]] (Imperial Ordinance No. 3, March 2, Meiji 19), the [[Shihan Gakkōrei]] (Ordinance No. 13, April 10, Meiji 19), the [[Shōgakkōrei]] (Ordinance No. 14, April 10, Meiji 19), the [[Chūgakkōrei]] (Ordinance No. 15, April 10, Meiji 19), and the [[Shogakkō Tsūsoku]] (General School Ordinance, Ordinance No. 16, April 10, Meiji 19). These constitute the "'''narrow definition of Gakkōrei'''" and replaced the [[Kyōikurei=Third Kyōikurei|Third Kyōikurei]].
In its "'''broader definition'''," Gakkōrei refers to the collective term for standalone ordinances that regulated specific school types promulgated sporadically until the [[School Education Act]] of 1947. Unlike the [[Gakusei]] and [[Kyōikurei]] (single comprehensive edicts), the Gakkōrei was not a single legal document but a collective designation for multiple ordinances. To emphasize this distinction, it is sometimes referred to as "Shogakkōrei."
== Development == === Transition from Gakusei and Kyōikurei === Initially, public education in [[:en:Modern Japan|Modern Japan]] was governed by the [[Gakusei]], established in [[:en:1872|1872]] (Meiji 5), and later replaced by the [[Kyōikurei]] in [[:en:1879|1879]]. These edicts comprehensively regulated various types of schools. However, the rapid expansion of the educational system in the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] necessitated significant reforms in the [[:en:1880s|1880s]]. The Kyōikurei underwent complete revisions twice, in [[:en:1880|1880]] and [[:en:1885|1885]], before being replaced by the Gakkōrei series in [[:en:1886|1886]].
=== Mori Arinori and the Creation of Gakkōrei === [[File:Arinori Mori 2.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Mori Arinori, the first [[:en:Minister of Education|Minister of Education]], who played a key role in establishing the Gakkōrei.]] [[:en:Mori Arinori|Mori Arinori]], Japan's first [[:en:Minister of Education|Minister of Education]], initiated the creation of Gakkōrei during his tenure. Mori believed that education reform should prioritize national stability and align with state objectives. Emphasizing teacher training, he advocated for the establishment of separate ordinances ("individual regulations") to avoid frequent large-scale revisions.
Consequently, between March and April of [[:en:1886|1886]], five standalone edicts were promulgated: the [[Teikoku Daigakurei]], [[Shihan Gakkōrei]], [[Shōgakkōrei]], [[Chūgakkōrei]], and [[Shogakkō Tsūsoku]]. These ordinances collectively replaced the [[Kyōikurei]] and established the foundation of Japan's [[public education system]] under state control. Mori planned to enact additional ordinances, but his assassination in [[:en:1889|1889]] left his vision incomplete.
=== Subsequent Revisions and Additions === The initial ordinances underwent revisions to adapt to Japan's changing educational needs. These revisions clarified the roles of local governments, redefined primary and secondary education, and expanded higher education. Notable reforms included the [[High School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1894|1894]], the [[Private School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1899|1899]], and the [[Specialized School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1903|1903]]. By the [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō era]], the educational system had become highly structured, culminating in the [[University Ordinance]] of [[:en:1918|1918]], which allowed the establishment of non-imperial [[:en:universities|universities]].
=== Wartime Education and Postwar Reforms === During the [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa era]], Japan's education system was heavily influenced by wartime demands. The [[Youth School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1935|1935]] and the [[Secondary School Ordinance]] of [[:en:1943|1943]] reflected efforts to mobilize the population. Following Japan's defeat in [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], the [[:en:Allied Occupation of Japan|Allied Occupation of Japan]] mandated educational reforms, leading to the enactment of the [[School Education Act]] in [[:en:1947|1947]], which replaced the old system with a unified modern structure.
== Related Ordinances (Narrow Definition) == === Imperial University Ordinance === {{main|Teikoku Daigakurei}} Established in [[:en:1886|1886]], the [[Teikoku Daigakurei]] defined the structure of [[:en:Imperial Universities|Imperial Universities]]. Subsequent revisions led to its renaming as the [[National Comprehensive University Ordinance]] in [[:en:1947|1947]].
=== Teacher Training Ordinance === {{main|Shihan Gakkōrei|Shihan Kyōikurei}} The [[Shihan Gakkōrei]] regulated [[:en:normal schools|normal schools]] and was later replaced by the [[Shihan Kyōikurei]] in [[:en:1897|1897]] to enhance teacher training.
=== Primary School Ordinance === {{main|Shōgakkōrei|Kokumin Gakkōrei}} The [[Shōgakkōrei]] established the structure of [[:en:primary education|primary education]], later replaced by the [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]] in [[:en:1941|1941]].
=== Middle School Ordinance === {{main|Chūgakkōrei|Chūtō Gakkōrei}} The [[Chūgakkōrei]] defined [[:en:middle schools|middle schools]] and evolved into the [[Chūtō Gakkōrei]] in [[:en:1943|1943]].
=== General School Ordinance === {{main|Shogakkō Tsūsoku}} The [[Shogakkō Tsūsoku]] regulated school facilities, including libraries and teacher qualifications. It was repealed in [[:en:1900|1900]].
== Broader Scope of Gakkōrei == === Specialized School Ordinance (1903) {{main|Specialized School Ordinance}} === University Ordinance (1918) {{main|University Ordinance}}

=== Teacher Training Ordinance === {{main|Shihan Gakkōrei|Shihan Kyōikurei}} The [[Shihan Gakkōrei]] regulated [[:en:normal schools|normal schools]] and was later replaced by the [[Shihan Kyōikurei]] in [[:en:1897|1897]] to enhance teacher training.
=== Primary School Ordinance === {{main|Shōgakkōrei|Kokumin Gakkōrei}} The [[Shōgakkōrei]] established the structure of [[:en:primary education|primary education]], later replaced by the [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]] in [[:en:1941|1941]].
=== Middle School Ordinance === {{main|Chūgakkōrei|Chūtō Gakkōrei}} The [[Chūgakkōrei]] defined [[:en:middle schools|middle schools]] and evolved into the [[Chūtō Gakkōrei]] in [[:en:1943|1943]].
=== General School Ordinance === {{main|Shogakkō Tsūsoku}} The [[Shogakkō Tsūsoku]] regulated school facilities, including libraries and teacher qualifications. It was repealed in [[:en:1900|1900]].
== Broader Scope of Gakkōrei == === Specialized School Ordinance (1903) {{main|Specialized School Ordinance}} === University Ordinance (1918) {{main|University Ordinance}}
